mnemonics: CHI-SQUARE: Calculates How Independent Something is, Quantifying Uncertainty And Relationship Evidence
keyPoints: The Chi-square test is used to determine if there is a significant association between two categorical variables.
It works by comparing observed frequencies in a contingency table to the frequencies expected if the variables were independent.
A low p-value (typically <0.05) indicates that the observed data is unlikely under the null hypothesis of independence, so we reject the null.
commonMistakes: Applying the Chi-square test to continuous data or data that isn't counts/frequencies.
Using the test when expected cell counts are too low (a common rule is that all expected counts should be greater than 5).
Confusing a significant result (rejecting the null) with a strong or large-effect association; significance does not measure the strength of the relationship.
